think its worth noting that the no. 29 overall selection (alex karaban) that the cavs traded to the sacramento kings will make $3.04m this season. if they draft a player at no. 34 they can pay as little as $1.35m this season creating a $1.69m cap savings. its unlikely theyll be able to get the no. 34 pick to agree to a two-way deal, so its possible that the cavs trade back again in search of a player that would be on that type of contract this season. if the cavs go that route, it would cost them nothing against the cap, because two-way contracts do not factor in. saving $3.04m might not sound like a lot, but if the cavs are trying to stay out of the second apron in order to create some roster building flexibility, every dollar counts.

lebron james has won his four nba championships across three different franchises: the miami heat (2012 and 2013), cleveland cavaliers (2016), and los angeles lakers (2020). he accomplished this after leaving his original team and frequently joining or assembling superteams on already competitive rosters. in contrast: kobe bryant won all five of his championships with a single team — the los angeles lakers — and never left. michael jordan won all six of his titles with the chicago bulls. his later playing stint with the washington wizards produced zero championships. stephen curry has won all four of his championships exclusively with the golden state warriors, the team that drafted him. kareem abdul-jabbar is the one exception in that group: he won one title with the milwaukee bucks (1971) and five more with the lakers after being traded there in 1975. while lebrons willingness to change teams helped him secure titles in different cities, the other three legends (kobe, jordan, and curry) achieved all of their success while staying loyal to one franchise for their entire championship runs. lakers is better off without him. hasta la vista trader!

former nba players malik beasley and edward davis were indicted monday on federal allegations they rigged games so online gamblers could get rich, making them the latest players to face charges in a widespread sports betting scandal. beasley, 29, most recently a guard with the detroit pistons before a gambling investigation derailed his free agency last year, and davis, 37, are charged alongside four others in brooklyn federal court with wire fraud conspiracy, bribery in sporting contests, honest services wire fraud conspiracy, and money laundering conspiracy. beasley is accused of fixing three games in 2024, when he played for the milwaukee bucks. davis, a former teammate of beasley on the minnesota timberwolves, acted as his gatekeeper, and beasley owed him gambling debts, according to federal prosecutors. davis last played in the nba for the cleveland cavaliers in 2022. the two decided which games beasley would underperform and overperform in when it came to statistics like rebounds and points scored, according to the indictment. in exchange, beasleys debts would be reduced or paid off, the feds allege. nydailynews.com 2026 06 29 f

i think this misses the point. technology built for business does not mean technology only for businesses. it means technology built for real products, real users, real distribution, and real-world adoption. in fact, businesses are often how consumer-scale adoption actually reaches people. sometimes that end user is a financial services institution. sometimes its a small business. and sometimes its millions of individuals accessing better products because a real company was able to build on better infrastructure. look at whats already happening on avalanche: consumer experiences with the cleveland cavaliers, detroit pistons, fifa and others; platforms like dinari giving people globally access to u.s. equities through a b2b2c model; payments, financial services, sports, ai, and consumer apps leveraged in production. the point is not enterprise over people. rather, its moving beyond crypto casinos and short-term speculation toward technology that enables companies, builders, and communities to launch products people actually use. of course culture matters. liquidity matters. community matters. but durable ecosystems are built when that energy connects with real utility, real distribution, and real businesses solving real problems. thats the avalanche differentiator and the avalanche bet.

had an epiphany: most people are tuning in to see wemby and his spurs for the first time. some are comparing these spurs to the last finals they watched (e.g. warriors cavs, spurs heat, etc) and are kinda offended by the gaps they see. the casual fan isnt wrong to point out how this spurs team lacks the maturity and sophistication we expect from championship teams but thats kinda the point: this team is so, so young. the spurs core might be 6-8 years away from peaking and they just beat a 64-win defending champ to make the nba finals. when shaq got swept in the 1995 nba finals, i could only see his flaws. i hadnt been around the game long enough to understand that he was only 22 and going to get much better for the next 6 years. well, 6 years later he had 2 titles under his belt and would get another 2 in the next 5 seasons. im not predicting how many championships the spurs will win. for me, the whole fun of it is watching to find out, and tracing their growth trajectory along the way.
